title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about archaeology and discovery. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Digging Up the Past
content.subtitle: An article about the methods and meaning of modern archaeology.
content.text:
The public image of the archaeologist — trowel in one hand, brushing sand from the surface of a golden artefact — captures almost nothing of what archaeology actually involves. Modern excavation is slow, methodical, and often unglamorous work, conducted less in pursuit of objects than in pursuit of context. [31] An archaeologist who finds a Roman coin but cannot describe where precisely in a stratified soil layer it was found has, in professional terms, found almost nothing. The object without its context is merely decoration. [/31]

This emphasis on context reflects a fundamental shift in how the discipline understands its purpose. Earlier generations of archaeologists, working in the tradition of the great Victorian collectors, were primarily interested in acquiring remarkable objects for museums and private collections. The process of extraction was secondary to the prize. [32] Contemporary archaeology has largely inverted this priority: what matters is not the object but the story, and the story lives in the soil, in the spatial relationships between objects, and in the traces left by ordinary life rather than extraordinary events. [/32]

The technologies available to modern archaeologists have expanded the discipline beyond recognition. Ground-penetrating radar and LiDAR — a remote sensing technology that uses laser pulses to map terrain — have allowed researchers to identify buried structures without touching the ground. [33] In 2018, LiDAR surveys over the Guatemalan jungle revealed a network of ancient Maya cities, roads, and agricultural systems of extraordinary scale that had been invisible to ground-level exploration for centuries. [/33] Entire civilisations are becoming legible without a trowel being lifted.

Yet the most significant shift in archaeology may be conceptual rather than technological. For most of its history, archaeology was a discipline concerned with elites: the temples, palaces, and tombs of kings and priests. The daily lives of ordinary people — what they ate, where they slept, how they organised domestic space — were largely ignored as too fragmentary to study. [34] Environmental archaeology, which analyses plant remains, animal bones, pollen, and soil chemistry, has fundamentally changed this, opening a window onto the lives of people who left no written record and built no monuments. [/34]

Archaeological interpretation is rarely straightforward. The same evidence can support multiple readings, and the history of the discipline is marked by cases where confident interpretations were overturned by new findings. [35] This uncertainty is not a weakness of the discipline but a sign of its intellectual honesty: archaeology, unlike myth, updates itself when new evidence arrives. [/35]

For the public, archaeology's deepest appeal may be less about what is found and more about what the act of looking implies: that the past is not sealed off from us but remains, in fragments, legible. [36] Every excavation is, at some level, an argument against forgetting — a demonstration that the people who came before us were as complex, as struggling, and as human as ourselves. [/36]

questions.question: The writer's comment that an archaeologist who cannot record a coin's precise location 'has found almost nothing' means that
options: A) coins are among the least significant artefacts that archaeologists discover.
B) the value of an artefact depends entirely on where it was found in the ground.
C) an object's meaning in archaeology comes from its relationship to its surroundings.
D) modern archaeologists are not sufficiently careful when recording their discoveries.
questions.explanation: The statement 'The object without its context is merely decoration' means an artefact only gains archaeological meaning through its relationship to its context.

questions.question: The writer contrasts Victorian and contemporary archaeology to show that
options: A) Victorian archaeologists were more skilled at finding important objects.
B) the focus of archaeology has shifted from acquiring objects to understanding stories.
C) modern museums contain fewer objects than those of the Victorian era.
D) contemporary archaeologists are more interested in ordinary life than in historical events.
questions.explanation: Contemporary archaeology 'inverted this priority': instead of objects, 'what matters is not the object but the story' — a shift from collection to understanding.

questions.question: The LiDAR survey of Guatemala is mentioned as an example of
options: A) how advanced technology can replace traditional archaeological methods entirely.
B) the scale of Maya civilisation compared to other ancient cultures.
C) how remote sensing technology can reveal things invisible to direct exploration.
D) a discovery that changed our understanding of how ancient cities were built.
questions.explanation: The survey revealed cities that 'had been invisible to ground-level exploration for centuries', showing how technology can reveal what direct observation could not.

questions.question: Environmental archaeology has been significant because it has
options: A) made traditional excavation methods unnecessary.
B) allowed archaeologists to study the lives of ordinary people who left no written records.
C) provided evidence that contradicts previously accepted historical accounts.
D) given archaeologists access to information about environmental conditions in the ancient world.
questions.explanation: Environmental archaeology 'opening a window onto the lives of people who left no written record and built no monuments' — allowing study of ordinary, unrecorded lives.

questions.question: The writer's attitude towards uncertainty in archaeological interpretation is
options: A) critical, as it undermines the credibility of archaeological findings.
B) accepting, seeing it as a sign of the discipline's intellectual integrity.
C) concerned, as it makes it difficult to communicate findings to the public.
D) dismissive, as most archaeological interpretations are eventually confirmed.
questions.explanation: The writer says uncertainty 'is not a weakness of the discipline but a sign of its intellectual honesty' — a clearly accepting and positive attitude.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the writer suggests that the deeper appeal of archaeology lies in
options: A) the excitement of uncovering valuable objects from the past.
B) the recognition that past peoples were fundamentally similar to us.
C) the opportunity to challenge accepted historical narratives.
D) the development of sophisticated technologies for exploring the ground.
questions.explanation: The writer says archaeology demonstrates 'that the people who came before us were as complex, as struggling, and as human as ourselves' — the recognition of shared humanity.
